content:
First we introduce convenient notation.  Label the trapezoid so that  
A=(−a/2,h),  D=(+a/2,h),  B=(−b/2,0),  C=(+b/2,0)  
with BC∥AD,  BC=b>AD=a,  legs AB=CD=L,  height =h.  Then  
 L=AB=√[(b−a)²/4+h²],  
 AC=√[(a+b)²/4+h²],  
and the two “side” legs are equal so it is indeed isosceles.  

1)  The perimeter of ABCD is  
 2L + a + b =120.  

2)  The perimeter of ΔABC is  
 AB + BC + CA = L + b + √[(a+b)²/4+h²] =114.  

3)  By placing coordinates and using the incenter‐formula one finds that the two incenters lie on the same horizontal line, and their horizontal separation is  
 |x₂−x₁| = b·[a +√(d²+h²) −√(e²+h²)]/(b+L+AC)  
where d=(a+b)/2, e=(b−a)/2.  Setting this equal to 16 and using  b+L+AC=114  leads, together with (1), (2), to the simple system  
 d+√(e²+h²)=60,  
 e+√(d²+h²)=54,  
and the incenter‐distance equation.  

Carrying this out (or more canonically, eliminating h² by subtracting) one eventually gets the numerical solution  
 b=48,  a=22,  h²=456.  

Hence the area of the trapezoid is  
 Area = ½·(a+b)·h = ½·(22+48)·√456 =35·√456=35·2√114=70√114.  

Thus in the form m√n we have m=70, n=114, and  
 100m+n=100·70+114=7000+114=7114.  

Answer:  \boxed{7114}.

 AC=√[(a+b)²/4+h²],  
and the two “side” legs are equal so it is indeed isosceles.
